actual_result: verification_pattern returned FIXED in report-2026-04-22T08-44 (first clean Mode 2 day after PROP-010 landed) and again in report-2026-04-23T11 (this run). All three grep conditions pass: build.js has `target === 'sync-workspace'` branch + `syncToWorkspace()` function, and decider-patches-and-selfapply.md references `node build.js sync-workspace`. Mode 2 dispatcher quick-check shows 0/5 stale files on 2026-04-22 and 0/5 on 2026-04-23 vs. 3/5 on 2026-04-21. FUSE drift is gone.
problem:
FUSE workspace drifts stale vs. git main on every decider self-apply commit. `build.js publish` is the ONLY mechanism that syncs git → FUSE for git-owned files (data/{wins,sections,uncounted-failures,predictions}.json, docs/index.html, prompts, build-scripts, etc.), but the decider self-apply path commits and pushes WITHOUT calling publish. Evidence: in the last 24h (2026-04-20T10:46Z → 2026-04-21T10:40Z), there were 25 commits on main; 3 of them (df4b231, 67b14d5, b4fa494) landed after the most recent build.js-publish-equivalent workspace sync and left 3 git-owned files (data/wins.json, data/sections.json, docs/index.html) stale on FUSE at dispatcher quick-check this morning. This is the 3rd consecutive Mode 2 tinker run triggered by the same class of drift — 2026-04-19 (5 files stale, 3 decider commits behind), 2026-04-20 (5 files stale, 3 decider commits behind), 2026-04-21 (3 files stale, 3 decider commits behind). Mode 2 self-fix is a 30-second `cp`, but the underlying fix has never been written.

Why it matters: integrity and social agents read docs/index.html, data/*.json, and docs/llms.txt from the FUSE workspace WITHOUT cross-checking GitHub. When FUSE is 3 decider commits stale, these agents audit pre-patch content — e.g. integrity flags a 'missing anchor' that was fixed two commits ago, social writes a discoverability report against outdated WIN counts. No hard failure yet, but the surface area grows every churn-and-burn day (decider at 2h cycle, ~20 commits/day).

Secondary: `workspace-sync` (every 4h) pushes workspace→git but does NOT pull git→workspace. Even if it did, a 4h cadence is longer than the decider's 2h cycle, so FUSE would still lag by 2 decider cycles on average. The right fix is to sync git→FUSE *as part of every push* (seconds, not hours).

proposed_fix.summary: Add a `sync-workspace` target to build.js that runs ONLY the git→workspace sync block (skips HTML/PDF regeneration and commit/push). Then have the decider invoke it after every successful `git push origin main`. The new target reuses the existing OWNERSHIP table and prompts-dir walker, so there's no duplication. Workspace-sync can optionally call it too as a 4h safety net, but the decider call is the primary fix (it closes the drift window from 4h to <1s).

files_to_modify.explanation: Add target validation and register the new 'sync-workspace' target.

Strip the stale comment block so the inline sync code can be replaced by a function call. NOTE: this find/replace targets the comment that lives BEFORE the inline sync block inside the publish branch; the comment about `runTolerant` and nothing-to-commit is relocated to attach to `runTolerant` itself in a separate edit (see next entry). If the implementer prefers, the runTolerant comment can be left in place and this find can be tightened to just the first paragraph.
Extract syncToWorkspace() above the main target dispatch and add a dedicated `sync-workspace` branch that calls it without commit/push.
Replace the inlined sync block inside `if (target === 'publish') { ... }` with a call to the extracted function. `publish` now composes: commit+push+syncToWorkspace().

files_to_modify.find:
git push origin main

# 3b. If ANY test fails → abandon and leave for human
files_to_modify.replace:
git push origin main

# PROP-010: immediately sync git→FUSE so agents reading from the workspace
# (integrity, social, poller) see the post-push content on their next run.
# This is a lightweight call — no HTML/PDF regen, no git ops, just file
# copies driven by build.js's OWNERSHIP table. A failure here does NOT
# roll back the push; the push already succeeded and the 4h workspace-sync
# cycle is the backstop. Log the outcome but do not abort on sync failure.
node build.js sync-workspace || echo "WARN: sync-workspace failed; workspace-sync will backfill within 4h."

# 3b. If ANY test fails → abandon and leave for human
files_to_modify.explanation: Insert a sync-workspace call immediately after the successful push. Non-fatal: if the sync fails (missing /sessions mount, ephemeral FUSE hiccup), the push has already landed and workspace-sync will backfill within 4h.

tradeoffs: Wins: (1) FUSE goes from up-to-4h-stale to <1s-stale on every decider push — eliminates the recurring Mode 2 trigger. (2) Integrity, social, poller all get fresh data without knowing anything changed. (3) Reuses existing OWNERSHIP + prompts-walker so behavior matches publish exactly (no new ownership logic). (4) Extraction makes the sync block independently testable — build.js has no unit tests today, but the refactor is a prerequisite for adding them. (5) Follows the same soft-fail pattern workspace-sync uses (log and move on). Costs / risks: (a) Every decider push now spawns one extra `node build.js sync-workspace` — ~100ms overhead, negligible at 2h cycle. (b) If the sync itself has a bug, it runs ~20x/day instead of ~2x/day, amplifying any regression. Mitigation: the function body is byte-for-byte what publish has always run; no new copy logic. (c) Couples decider to build.js — decider already depends on test.js and build-scripts/apply-patches.js, so this is in keeping with existing coupling. (d) Does not help if decider's clone is stale — but decider always clones fresh and pulls --rebase before writing, so the clone IS main at push time. (e) Extraction + target-validation adds ~15 lines net to build.js.
estimated_impact: Eliminates Mode 2 FUSE-staleness triggers (currently 1/day, 3 consecutive runs). Saves ~1 tinker run per day of 'mechanical Mode 2 self-fix + reiterate PROP-010 recommendation' → frees tinker for Mode 3 cost engineering. Removes a whole class of 'integrity audits stale HTML' risk. Reduces reliance on workspace-sync's 4h cycle as a belt-and-suspenders fix. Zero cost to agents that don't push (poller, analyst, integrity, social, tinker).
implementation_notes: Apply the four `files_to_modify` edits in order. After the last edit, run `node test.js` in the clone to confirm nothing broke. Smoke test: `node build.js sync-workspace` from any clone with a /sessions/<name>/mnt/dome-model-review mount should log `✅ Sync to workspace (...)` and exit 0 without touching git. Idempotent: running it twice in a row should report the same counts.
verification_pattern: grep -q "target === 'sync-workspace'" build.js && grep -q 'syncToWorkspace()' build.js && grep -q 'node build.js sync-workspace' monitor/prompts/reference/decider-patches-and-selfapply.md && echo FIXED || echo STILL_BROKEN

counter_arguments.claim: Just fix the decider to invoke `node build.js publish` on every self-apply commit — no new target needed.
counter_arguments.counter: publish regenerates HTML + PDF (Playwright headless Chromium for PDF). PDF generation is ~15s; HTML is ~2s; plus git staging and commit. At the decider's 2h cycle that's an extra ~20s * 12 = 4 minutes of wall-clock per day on regeneration alone, and the decider already regenerates HTML from wins.json via a targeted sed/write path in apply-patches.js. A lightweight sync-only target matches what the decider actually needs: copy already-built files from clone to FUSE. publish is for the nightly/manual build path and should stay heavyweight.

counter_arguments.claim: Extend workspace-sync to do bidirectional sync instead of adding a decider call.
counter_arguments.counter: workspace-sync runs every 4h. Decider runs every 2h with 1-3 commits per run. Between workspace-sync invocations, FUSE can be up to 8 decider pushes behind. The proposed fix closes the window to <1s at the push site, which is where the new state actually appears. Bidirectional workspace-sync would help but is additive, not a substitute. Also: workspace-sync's job today is conceptually clean (one direction, workspace→git); adding a reverse direction doubles its failure modes. Better to keep it single-direction and put the git→FUSE sync at the push site.

counter_arguments.claim: Decider doesn't need to sync FUSE — it clones fresh anyway, and the only consumers are integrity/social/poller. Fix those agents to read from a clone instead.
counter_arguments.counter: Partially true. Curmudgeon, decider, and analyst (on the Mode 0 pre-write cross-check) do clone or cross-check; those three are FUSE-staleness-immune. Integrity, social, and poller read from FUSE and would need clones of their own to become immune. Each clone is ~76MB and takes a few seconds; adding three more clone-per-run agents would increase disk pressure (already at 82%) and add scheduler complexity. The PROP-010 approach is strictly cheaper: one extra sync call at push time covers ALL FUSE readers for free. If disk pressure drops later, per-agent clones remain an option.

counter_arguments.claim: This introduces a new failure mode: sync runs ~20x/day instead of ~2x/day, amplifying any regression in the sync code.
counter_arguments.counter: The sync block is being extracted verbatim — no logic change. Frequency goes up, but the blast radius per run is identical. The soft-fail on the decider side (`|| echo WARN`) means a hypothetical regression logs a warning and leaves the FUSE in whatever state it's in; it does NOT break the push, does NOT cascade into tests, does NOT change any committed data. Workspace-sync's 4h cycle remains as a backstop regardless. Net risk is bounded by the existing publish path, which has been stable for weeks.

counter_arguments.claim: This should be a mechanical self-fix, not a PROP.
counter_arguments.counter: Per tinker-proposals-and-fixes.md Step 1, self-fixes are scoped to 'stale references, wrong paths, missing fields, typos.' This adds a new build target, refactors 110+ lines from inline to function, and modifies an agent prompt's push path. That's workflow redesign, which is exactly what Step 2 (PROPs) is for. The previous two Mode 2 runs flagged this as PROP-owed, not self-fixable.

escalation_history.action: Identified drift; applied cp self-fix; flagged PROP-010 as owed
Re-identified drift (3 decider commits since last sync); applied cp self-fix; flagged PROP-010 as priority-1 next-Mode-4 work
